attributes.stem: A 63-year-old man presents with a one day history of a new onset of severe, dull aching right sided headache and retro-orbital pain that developed gradually over several hours. He gives history of being hit on the neck by ball whilst playing tennis three days ago, but otherwise has an unremarkable medical history. He is a non-smoker and denies any history of previous ocular, cardiac, vascular, or neurological disease. His blood pressure 142/74 mmHg, pulse 90/min, respiratory rate 16/min and temperature 37.4oC. Examination of his eyes is shown (Image). A neurological examination is normal. CT brain is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial imaging investigation?
options: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) of head and neck vessels
attributes.explanation:
The appearance in the patient shows ptosis and miosis, consistent with Horner's syndrome. The history of aching headache and eye pain following blunt trauma suggests carotid dissection. Most such patients experience neck, facial, and head pain ipsilateral to the lesion because of ischaemia or stretching of the trigeminal pain fibres surrounding the carotid arteries. Horner's syndrome is present in up to 58% of internal carotid artery dissections and it is commonly painful (90% of cases). Internal carotid artery dissection is a potentially life-threatening condition and carries a substantial risk of disabling stroke (30% of patients will have a stroke, on average six days after the onset). In carotid dissection a CT brain is usually normal unless there are clinical findings suggestive of an established stroke.

Digital subtraction angiography is the gold standard for carotid artery dissection, but it is invasive and carries a 1-2% risk of stroke itself.

CT angiography is considered the primary imaging modality for carotid dissection, as it is non-invasive and has a sensitivity and specificity of >95%. MR angiography (rather than MRI brain) is also a valid non invasive imaging option with reasonable sensitivity and specificity. However for acute dissections, the mural haematoma consists primarily of deoxyhaemoglobin, which does not have a hyperintense T1-weighted signal and can easily be missed.

Duplex ultrasound is of limited value because, while it is non-invasive, it is unable to visualise the entire carotid artery (it excludes the intracranial portion - a common site of dissection).

CT chest would detect apical lung tumour (Pancoast tumour) causing Horner's syndrome, however in non-smoker with absent chest symptoms, a lung tumour is less likely. The history of trauma, headache and orbital pain makes carotid dissection a more appropriate diagnosis.
learningPoints: Painful Horner syndrome should alert clinicians to the possibility of a silent carotid dissection until proven otherwise.
references: Kasravi N, Leung A, Silver I, Burneo JG. Dissection of the internal carotid artery causing Horner syndrome and palsy of cranial nerve XII. CMAJ 2010;182:E373–7.
